title: Silverpine SDK orchestration & VPC quickstart - integration kickoff
next_steps: Alex to send SOC 2 summary + KMS integration notes (by 2025-01-09)
Priya to provision a sandbox with VPC peering guide and example terraform (by 2025-01-10)
Maya/Eric to share sample inference traffic pattern and peak qps (by 2025-01-08)
Follow-up bench demo (canary throughput) scheduled week of 2025-01-13
action_items: Alex Chen - email SOC2 certificate and links to security docs (due 2025-01-09)
Priya Nair - create sandbox project + sample terraform + quickstart script (due 2025-01-10)

transcript:
Header:
Date/Time: 2025-01-06 15:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes
Attendees: Alex Chen (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Maya Ortiz (Staff ML Eng - Silverpine), Tom Ratcliffe (CTO - Silverpine), Eric Wu (Infra Eng - Silverpine)

Summary:
Quickstart / integration kickoff to walk Silverpine through Redwood SDKs (Python + TypeScript), auth and environment setup for a VPC/private deployment. Goal: get a sandbox up, validate auth flow (KMS + rotation), and run a small throughput/latency bench next week.

Topics covered:
- Quickstart flow for SDK (py/ts) and examples
- Auth: API keys, rotation, KMS/HSM, Workload Identity for GCP
- VPC peering and private control plane considerations
- CI/CD secrets and token rotation best practices
- Console + Optimize features (batching/caching suggestions)
- Next steps: probational sandbox, security docs, benchmark run

Action items (captured in metadata above).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Alex Chen: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Alex from Redwood, and with me is Priya, our solutions engineer. Um, quick roll call?
[00:08] Maya Ortiz: Maya here.
[00:09] Tom Ratcliffe: Tom here.
[00:10] Eric Wu: Eric on infra.
[00:12] Priya Nair: Great, thanks. Today's, uh, agenda is to walk through the SDK quickstart, focus on auth and environment setup since you requested VPC/private details, and then we'll sketch out the bench. Sound good?
[00:23] Tom Ratcliffe: Yep sounds good. We're mostly focused on private deployment or VPC peering rather than public hosted at this stage.
[00:30] Alex Chen: Totally. Quick note — we support three modes: hosted API, dedicated reserved capacity, and Private with VPC options. For the SDK quickstart we'll show the standard API key flow plus the recommended private wiring.
[00:42] Maya Ortiz: Quick Q: our infra team wants KMS-backed keys and automatic rotation, plus audit logs. Is that in the roadmap or immediate?
[00:50] Priya Nair: Yes — we have KMS integrations and HSM support for Private customers. For hosted we do delegated key management options, and for Private you can bring your KMS. Rotation is supported; the recommended pattern is short-lived tokens issued by your KMS with Redwood API keys swapped via CI. We'll show an example.
[01:10] Eric Wu: And for GCP specifically, is workload identity recommended instead of service account keys?
[01:16] Priya Nair: Exactly — Workload Identity + short-lived tokens is preferred. In our sample terraform we demonstrate the GCP workload identity path to issue service tokens to the SDK container.
[01:26] Tom Ratcliffe: Cool. Walk us through the python SDK flow first?
[01:30] Priya Nair: Yep. So on the screen I have the quickstart repo — the README shows pip install redwood-sdk, then set REDWOOD_API_KEY or use a local credentials file. The typical pattern: create a client with the api key, call client.generate(...) for text, or embeddings for vectors. For private deployments you point the SDK at the private control plane endpoint, which we show as an env var REDWOOD_BASE_URL.
[01:55] Maya Ortiz: I'm worried about accidental public egress — if our app uses the SDK and the base URL is misconfigured, it could talk to hosted. Any guardrails?
[02:04] Alex Chen: Good point. We recommend enforcing a hostname allowlist at the network layer and also using client-side validation of REDWOOD_BASE_URL in the container startup. For Private, the control plane is in your VPC or peered network so network-level routing prevents accidental egress. We'll include a snippet in the quickstart to validate the env var.
[02:24] Eric Wu: Will the SDK retry or cache tokens? We prefer the token exchange to be explicit in our entrypoint.
[02:30] Priya Nair: The SDK has optional helpers for token refresh but we expose a low-level auth hook so your entrypoint can manage token rotation. If you want to roll-your-own, use token_provider callback — it's in the python and TS libs. Note: the python helper will cache the token for a short TTL to avoid excess calls, but you can disable caching.
[02:50] Tom Ratcliffe: Nice. Can you show the ts example as well — we have a node service for ingestion.
[02:55] Priya Nair: Sure. In TypeScript it's similar: npm i @redwood/sdk, set process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY or use the client constructor with a token provider. For server-side node we recommend Workload Identity or AWS IAM role chaining rather than static keys.
[03:12] Maya Ortiz: Any gotchas for local developer machines? We want local dev to be easy but we can't expose production keys.
[03:18] Alex Chen: We suggest two profiles: dev and prod. Dev uses short-lived sandbox tokens with limited scope and quota; we can provision sandbox API keys for your dev team that are rate-limited. Priya will provision a sandbox today.
[03:30] Priya Nair: I'll set up a sandbox project and create a terraform module for VPC peering and an example docker-compose for local dev that uses workload identity emulation.
[03:39] Eric Wu: About VPC peering: we have existing peering with some partners and limited CIDR space. What's the minimal requirement?
[03:46] Priya Nair: Minimal setup is a single peering connection and route entries for control-plane subnets. For Private there's an option for private-link style endpoint or routing via peering. If peering is hard, we can use TLS+mutual auth across a dedicated egress IP as workaround. We'll need your CIDR and existing routes.
[04:06] Tom Ratcliffe: Understood. Let's talk about performance: we care about p95 latency under 200ms for short prompts and cost per token. Can Redwood meet that?
[04:14] Alex Chen: For those SLOs the usual pattern is using dedicated pools with lower batching windows. We can tune batch size and KV cache settings in Optimize to reduce tail latency. For short prompts we often recommend a quantized model variant with smaller kernels. Pricing tradeoff — lower latency increases cost. We can run a bench to show the latency/cost curve.
[04:39] Maya Ortiz: Is that something you run in our sandbox or hosted?
[04:42] Priya Nair: We can run it in either. For accurate network path we prefer running in your VPC sandbox. We'll bring a small harness that emits the sample traffic you share.
[04:52] Alex Chen: If possible, send us token distribution and typical sequence lengths so the bench can use realistic payloads.
[04:58] Maya Ortiz: Will do.
[05:00] Tom Ratcliffe: Security checklist — we need SOC2, ISO, SAML SSO integration, audit logs retention options.
[05:07] Alex Chen: We are SOC 2 Type II; our security pack includes SOC docs, pen test summary, and a compliance checklist. SAML/SSO and RBAC are supported in Console for admin controls. Audit log retention configurable on Private. I'll send the pack after this call.
[05:22] Maya Ortiz: Great. Also need to understand data residency — our users are EU-only for some projects.
[05:28] Priya Nair: We have EU regions and Private can be deployed in your on-prem or VPC region. We'll include region options in the proposal.
[05:35] Eric Wu: Can you outline the CI/CD secret rotation pattern? We want to avoid long-lived keys in our repos.
[05:41] Priya Nair: Best practice: store a short bootstrap token in the CI that requests a short-lived key from your KMS or identity service, then inject the short token into the runtime environment. Redwood SDK supports using an external token provider to accept those short tokens. We have sample GitHub Actions and GitLab CI steps in the repo.
[05:58] Tom Ratcliffe: Are there sample terraform modules for the peering + route config?
[06:03] Priya Nair: Yes, I'll attach a minimal terraform example. It includes VPC peering, route table entries, and a small example to create firewall rules for the control plane endpoints.
[06:15] Maya Ortiz: On the SDK side — does the TS SDK support streaming and function/tool calling? We plan to use streaming webhooks for agent-like features.
[06:23] Priya Nair: Yes, both SDKs support streaming responses and function calling. For server-side streaming we have examples using server-sent events and websockets. The TS SDK includes a stream iterator helper.
[06:36] Alex Chen: Quick demo time — Priya, can you share the screen and walk through the python quickstart?
[06:40] Priya Nair: Sure — pulling up the quickstart repo. (screen share) First steps: pip install redwood-sdk==1.4.2, export REDWOOD_BASE_URL and REDWOOD_API_KEY. Then run examples/hello_generate.py. The sample uses small prompt and prints tokens as they stream.
[07:05] Priya Nair: Note the env var REDWOOD_PROFILE=dev will switch to the sandbox token provider we provision. Also we show how to configure client.token_provider for GCP Workload Identity.
[07:18] Maya Ortiz: I see a mention of KV cache — is that automatic?
[07:21] Priya Nair: We have optional prefix caching for repeated context that you can enable per-client. It's designed to reduce compute for repeated few-shot contexts. In Optimize you can simulate cache hit rates to estimate cost savings.
[07:35] Tom Ratcliffe: For our pipeline we plan to use both embeddings and generation. Any guidance on embedding models and sharding?
[07:42] Alex Chen: Embeddings are treated as batchable requests; use smaller batch sizes to reduce latency if needed. We support sharded embedding endpoints for high throughput. We'll include a suggested config for batch size per GPU in the sandbox docs.
[07:58] Eric Wu: A couple of small things — do you rotate API keys automatically or do we handle rotation entirely?
[08:04] Priya Nair: For Private, you control KMS and rotation; Redwood supports automatic key rollover if you enable integration. For hosted we manage rotation and you get a rotated key with a webhooks notification option.
[08:18] Maya Ortiz: What about usage analytics and per-route cost breakdown? Ops wants to see token-level cost.
[08:23] Alex Chen: Console shows per-route breakdown, token producer breakdown, and per-token estimated cost. For Private we can feed the same telemetry to your SIEM via log drain.
[08:34] Tom Ratcliffe: Any limitation we should know about on model versions and rollouts?
[08:38] Priya Nair: You can pin model versions and use canary rollouts in Console. We support automatic fallback to a compatible variant in case of capacity issues.
[08:49] Alex Chen: Ok we're about 9 minutes in — sorry I mean 40 minutes — time check: we've covered core items. Priya will provision sandbox, I'll send security docs, and we should sync on bench inputs.
[08:59] Maya Ortiz: Quick question on billing: does dedicated reserved capacity have a minimum?
[09:05] Alex Chen: Yes reserved pools have a commitment term and minimum capacity; we'll include options in the pricing doc. For pilots we often do a small dedicated pool or run on hosted to calibrate.
[09:16] Eric Wu: For the bench next week, can you run a few scenarios: 50 qps short prompts, 200 qps batched embeddings, and a p95 latency curve?
[09:24] Priya Nair: Absolutely. We'll need your sample payloads and target metrics, then we'll run in the sandbox.
[09:30] Tom Ratcliffe: Any concerns about vendor lock-in with the SDK? We don't want to be tied to proprietary hooks.
[09:36] Alex Chen: SDKs are mostly thin HTTP clients; you can swap underlying endpoints. For Private we recommend standard APIs so you can migrate if required. We'll document the HTTP contract.
[09:46] Maya Ortiz: OK. Last bit — do you have a checklist for onboarding security review? I can send to our legal/compliance.
[09:52] Alex Chen: Yes — I'll attach a one-pager with SOC2, SAML, encryption-at-rest/in-transit, KMS integration steps, and audit log retention options.
[09:59] Tom Ratcliffe: Thanks. That should help.
[10:02] Alex Chen: Any final questions?
[10:05] Maya Ortiz: No, thanks.
[10:07] Eric Wu: One tiny thing — how do we get logs of inference requests for debugging?
[10:11] Priya Nair: We can enable request logging with PII redaction; logs can be shipped to your GCP logging or S3. We'll include a sample.
[10:20] Alex Chen: Great. Recap of next steps: I'll send SOC2 + security pack and pricing; Priya will provision the sandbox + terraform + quickstart scripts; Maya/Eric will send traffic patterns; we'll schedule bench week of Jan 13.
[10:33] Tom Ratcliffe: Sounds good, thanks everyone.
[10:36] Alex Chen: Thanks all, we'll follow up by email.

Notes (imperfect transcription artifacts):
- Occasionally speaker labels overlapped; parts of the sample code snippet were transcribed as "dot dot redwood" instead of redwood-sdk in places.
- "KV cache" was sometimes transcribed as "KV cash" in auto transcript.
- Some timestamps/cues were approximate.

Shared links mentioned on call:
- Quickstart repo (demo): https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-quickstart (example link)
- Sandbox provisioning guide (internal): https://redwood.example/sandbox-setup (demo link)
- Security pack: https://redwood.example/security/silverpine-pack (will be delivered)
- Bench plan (template): https://redwood.example/bench/bench-template.xlsx

End of transcript.
